key_insight: A first-principles multiscale model accurately predicts hydrogen diffusion and retention in irradiated bcc metals by treating traps as multi-occupancy defects rather than single-occupancy sites.
engineering_relevance: The framework enables predictive simulation of hydrogen behavior in radiation-damaged tungsten and vanadium, supporting better lifetime and embrittlement assessment for fusion and nuclear materials.
method: Molecular dynamics to compute effective hydrogen diffusivity in monovacancies and voids
Lattice kinetic Monte Carlo to verify rapid convergence to steady-state trap occupancy
Analytic multi-occupancy-trap theory compared against atomistic simulation without fitted parameters
evidence_signal: The abstract reports good agreement between full atomistic simulations and the analytic diffusivity model across monovacancies in tungsten and vanadium and voids in tungsten, validating the theory for nanoscale radiation-damage defects.
watch_item: The approach depends on physically computed defect and trap parameters, so accuracy will hinge on the quality of the underlying density functional theory or interatomic potential descriptions of the irradiated microstructure.
paper_title: Multiscale modelling of diffusion and retention of hydrogen in multi-occupancy traps in irradiated bcc metals
